news_item.title: Godot will no longer accept AI-authored code contributions

insight: Godot's new policy — rejecting AI-authored code because contributors "heavy users of AI" often can't explain or fix what was generated — is a useful check for anyone building an agent-driven workflow like this one. As Phase 1 of the AI Lab stands up Hermes skills, the Journal, and the Infinite Context Engine, the real bar isn't whether an agent can produce a skill or a cron job, it's whether the human running the system can still read, debug, and own every piece it outputs. Worth building that review discipline into the vault now, before automation outpaces understanding: https://www.pcgamer.com/gaming-industry/open-source-game-engine-godot-will-no-longer-accept-ai-authored-code-contributions-we-cant-trust-heavy-users-of-ai-to-understand-their-code-enough-to-fix-it/

verdict.critique: The artifact directly ties a real news event (Godot's AI-code policy) to the project's actual Phase 1 components — Hermes skills, cron jobs, Journal, Infinite Context Engine — named explicitly in context.md and goals.md, so relevance is strong and earned rather than asserted. It reads clearly, makes a genuine point (produce vs. understand/debug) rather than a forced analogy, and comes in at roughly 96 words, just under the limit. The main weaknesses are stylistic: three em-dash interruptions in two sentences make it slightly clunky, and it ends on a vague call to action ('worth building that review discipline') without specifying what that discipline would actually look like in the vault.
improvements: Cut one of the em-dash asides to tighten the opening sentence and reduce clutter.
Replace the vague closing ('worth building that review discipline') with a concrete next step, e.g. a specific review checklist item to add before shipping a skill.
Trim a few more words to leave margin under the 100-word cap rather than landing right at the edge.
